skill.name: convex-quickstart
skill.description: Set up a new Convex project or add Convex to an existing frontend with framework-specific provider and environment configuration guidance.
skill.summary: Guides Convex project scaffolding, frontend integration, development setup, and first query or mutation verification.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and heuristic flags, but review shows the skill is setup documentation for Convex rather than hidden executable code. Package-manager and Convex CLI commands are legitimate for the stated purpose, but they still install dependencies, contact external services, and write local project configuration, so publication should include a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate Package Manager and Convex CLI Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users or agents to run npm and Convex CLI commands. This is expected for Convex setup, but it can install third-party packages, create files, start long-running processes, authenticate with Convex, and deploy backend code if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and central to the setup workflow. They are not malicious, but the security impact of package installation and deployment commands is real.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation and Localhost URLs
low_findings.description: The network findings point to local development ports and official Convex documentation links. No evidence found that the skill sends user data or secrets to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are localhost references or docs.convex.dev links. There is no request code or exfiltration instruction in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Environment File References Are Setup Instructions
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file findings refer to .env.local and public Convex deployment URL variables used by frontend frameworks. The skill does not instruct the agent to read or exfiltrate secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is framework configuration and verification. The variables shown are Convex URL values, not private API keys or credential-harvesting instructions.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detector Matched Non-Crypto Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are false positives in descriptive text and YAML metadata. No cryptographic algorithm selection or hashing code is present in the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found descriptive Convex setup text and documentation references at these lines. There is no crypto API or weak algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detector Matched a Relative Import Example
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding is a TypeScript import path that points from a React component to generated Convex API types. It is not file-system traversal using user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is a static import example inside documentation. It does not read files or construct paths dynamically.

low_findings.title: SQLite Database Detector Matched Convex Database API Usage
low_findings.description: The SQLite findings are false positives caused by Convex database API examples that query and insert records. No local SQLite database file is referenced.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show Convex query and mutation examples using ctx.db. They are unrelated to SQLite files.

low_findings.title: Heuristic Dangerous Combination Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined code execution, network, and credential heuristic is not confirmed as malicious. The cited behavior is expected setup guidance with explicit notes telling the agent to ask the user to run interactive Convex commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same file contains commands, URLs, and environment references, but review found no hidden execution path or data exfiltration intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Installation and Deployment Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands that install packages, scaffold a project, run the Convex development loop, and deploy to production. Users should review commands and templates before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These commands are clearly present and have security relevance. Their intent is legitimate setup, not stealth execution.

content.value_statement: Starting Convex can require several framework-specific steps and environment settings. This skill guides scaffolding, provider setup, development server handoff, and first function verification.
actual_capabilities: Chooses between a new Convex project and adding Convex to an existing app.
Recommends official Convex templates for React, Next.js, Clerk, Convex Auth, and backend-only projects.
Provides setup steps for ConvexProvider in React Vite and Next.js App Router apps.
Explains required Convex environment variable names for common frontend frameworks.
Guides verification with generated types, schema, query, mutation, and frontend hook examples.
Documents headless agent mode for anonymous local Convex development.
limitations: It does not replace the official Convex CLI or complete browser login for the user.
It focuses on initial setup, not advanced schema design or production architecture.
It assumes a JavaScript or TypeScript frontend workflow using npm-compatible tooling.
It includes command examples that users should review before running.

use_cases.title: Prototype a New Full-Stack App
use_cases.description: Create a Convex-backed React or Next.js starter and verify the first query and mutation quickly.

use_cases.title: Add Convex to an Existing Frontend
use_cases.description: Install Convex, wire the provider, set the deployment URL, and confirm generated client types.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Agent-Friendly Dev Environment
use_cases.description: Use anonymous agent mode or user-run development commands when browser authentication is not available.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Convex App
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a new Convex app for a simple React prototype. Pick the best starter template and tell me which commands I need to run.

prompt_templates.title: Add Convex to This App
prompt_templates.prompt: Add Convex to this existing frontend project. Identify the framework, install what is needed, and wire the Convex provider correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Verify the Development Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether Convex is set up correctly. Verify generated files, environment variables, and a basic query and mutation workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Headless Agent Mode
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare Convex development for a headless agent environment. Avoid browser login and explain any commands that I must approve or run.

output_examples.input: Set up Convex for a new React prototype.
output: Recommends the React Vite shadcn template for a fast prototype.
Lists the scaffold and install commands for user review.
Explains that the user should run the Convex dev loop because it may require login.
Describes the generated Convex folder and environment file to verify.

output_examples.input: Add Convex to my Next.js app.
output: Installs the Convex package and adds a client provider component.
Uses the Next.js public Convex URL environment variable.
Wraps the application layout with the provider.
Adds a small query and mutation verification plan.

output_examples.input: I am using a cloud coding agent with no browser login.
output: Explains anonymous agent mode for local development.
Shows which environment setting is needed.
Separates commands the agent can prepare from commands the user should approve.

best_practices: Ask the user to run interactive Convex commands when browser login may be required.
Create Convex clients at module scope so components do not recreate clients on render.
Verify setup by checking generated types, environment variables, and one working query or mutation.
anti_patterns: Do not run production deployment commands during ordinary local development.
Do not hardcode Convex deployment URLs in source files when framework environment variables are available.
Do not add Convex auth setup with this skill when the project already has Convex initialized.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete Convex app?
faq.answer: It guides creation or integration using official Convex setup flows. The user may still need to run CLI commands locally.

faq.question: Can the agent run npx convex dev automatically?
faq.answer: The skill recommends that users run it because first-time setup may require browser authentication and starts a long-running watcher.

faq.question: Which frontend frameworks are covered?
faq.answer: It covers React Vite and Next.js directly, and points users to Convex quickstarts for Vue, Svelte, Remix, React Native, and Node.js.

faq.question: Does it handle environment variables?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains the Convex URL variable names used by common frameworks and notes that the CLI writes them during setup.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production deployments?
faq.answer: It includes production deployment guidance, but users should review and approve deployment commands before running them.

faq.question: Does it include database examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a simple schema, query, mutation, and frontend usage example to verify the setup.
